graph.confusable_reason: Both are storage/postharvest rots of sweet potato roots that cause decay during curing and storage, and growers often lump "the roots are rotting in storage" together. Early lesions on a single root can be ambiguous, and the two can even co-occur (Rhizopus frequently follows wounding or other infections). The resolving features are the rot texture (dry/firm vs. wet/mushy), the smell, and the diagnostic signs (gray-black ringed lesions and dark fungal stroma vs. coarse gray-black "whiskery" sporangial mold).
graph.shared_presentation: Decaying sweet potato storage roots, often appearing during or after curing/storage, with darkening tissue and a soft or rotted feel to the affected root, leading growers to report generic "root rot in storage."

forks.question: Is the rot firm and relatively dry, or wet, soft, and mushy?
forks.a_signal: Firm, fairly dry, dark greenish-black to brown rot; flesh stays comparatively solid and the root may dry/mummify rather than collapse
forks.b_signal: Soft, watery, mushy rot that quickly collapses; root becomes a wet, leaking, stringy mass

forks.question: How fast does the whole root break down?
forks.a_signal: Relatively slow-spreading; circular cankers enlarge over days to weeks and may remain localized on the root surface and into flesh
forks.b_signal: Very rapid; an entire root and adjacent roots can liquefy within a few days, spreading root-to-root in the crate

forks.question: What does the surface mold/sign look like under good light or a hand lens?
forks.a_signal: Dark, gray-black lesion with concentric zonation; later fine dark fungal stroma and long-necked perithecia (flask-shaped fruiting bodies) producing sticky spore masses; no coarse whiskers
forks.b_signal: Coarse, whiskery gray-to-black mold with visible pinhead sporangia (black dots on white-gray stalks), like bread mold, often bridging between roots

forks.question: What is the smell and any liquid associated with the decay?
forks.a_signal: Comparatively mild or musty odor; little free liquid; tissue darkens uniformly
forks.b_signal: Strong sour/fermented (yeasty, alcoholic-vinegary) odor with abundant watery exudate

forks.question: Beyond the storage root, are there field/transplant symptoms on stems, slips, or below-soil stem (collar)?
forks.a_signal: Yes; Ceratocystis also causes black lesions on underground stems, blackening of slips/sprouts and 'black shank'-like collar lesions, and can move on infected seed roots and slips
forks.b_signal: No true field phase; Rhizopus is essentially a wound-entry postharvest rot of harvested roots and does not produce slip/stem disease in the field

forks.question: What conditions and wounds preceded the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Favored by infected seed roots/slips and storage; spreads via contaminated handling, can infect with less obvious wounding and persists in soil/storage houses
forks.b_signal: Strongly tied to bruising/wounding at harvest or washing plus warm, humid storage and poor curing; chilling injury and skinning predispose roots

graph.narrative_a: Black rot, caused by the fungus Ceratocystis fimbriata, produces firm, dark greenish-black to brown circular lesions on storage roots that often show concentric ring zonation. The rot stays comparatively dry and the flesh remains fairly solid, sometimes mummifying. Under a hand lens the lesion surface develops dark fungal stroma and long-necked, flask-shaped perithecia exuding sticky spore masses. Importantly, black rot has a field/transplant phase: it discolors and bitters the flesh, blackens underground stems and slips, and is readily carried on infected seed roots and plant beds, making sanitation and clean seed central to control.
graph.narrative_b: Rhizopus soft rot ("ring rot" or whiskery soft rot), caused by Rhizopus species, is a wound-entry postharvest disease. Spores germinate in bruises, skinning injuries, or cut surfaces and rapidly turn the root into a soft, watery, mushy mass that leaks fluid and gives off a strong sour, fermented (yeasty/alcoholic) odor. A coarse gray-to-black whiskery mold with pinhead black sporangia grows over and between roots like bread mold, allowing fast root-to-root spread within a crate. It is favored by warm, humid storage, poor curing, and rough handling; there is no true field disease phase.
graph.management_a: Plant only certified, disease-free seed roots and bed clean (non-infested) plant beds; cut slips above the soil line rather than pulling to avoid carrying the pathogen on root tissue. Rotate out of sweet potato for 2-3+ years, sanitize storage houses and handling equipment, and discard/cull infected roots. Cure properly and keep storage clean; consider approved seed-root or slip treatments and resistant-tolerant cultivars where available.
graph.management_b: Minimize wounding and bruising at harvest, washing, and grading; handle roots gently and avoid skinning. Cure promptly and correctly (about 29-32 C, ~85-90% RH for several days) to heal wounds, then store cool (~13-16 C) and avoid chilling injury. Maintain good ventilation and sanitation, remove rotting roots quickly, and use registered postharvest fungicide dips (e.g., dicloran where labeled) on wash water/handling lines when needed.

lay.farmer_lay_report: Some of my stored sweet potatoes are rotting. The skin and flesh are turning dark and the bad spots feel soft and mushy, and it's been showing up after I cured and put them away for storage.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look for dark gray-to-black sunken patches that have ring-like circles in them, often with a sticky, gummy goo, but NO coarse whiskers or fuzz standing up off the root.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Look for coarse, whiskery gray-to-black mold (like the fuzzy mold on old bread) with tiny black pinheads on white-gray stalks; it often grows strands that bridge from one root to the next.
